items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.
Students confuse storage with circulation. Blood carries glucose; muscles and liver store glycogen (the storage form). Liver glycogen regulates blood sugar; muscle glycogen fuels local contraction.

Students lump all training together. Plyometrics specifically targets POWER - explosive jumps and bounds use rapid eccentric-then-concentric muscle action, training the stretch-shortening cycle for fast force production.

items.q: During the upward phase of a biceps curl, which muscle is the agonist?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Biceps. “Triceps” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which contraction type occurs during a plank hold?
items.why: It's Isometric. “Concentric isotonic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many muscles are needed to form one antagonistic pair?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
